items.correct: An object's design should be determined by its purpose.
items.misconception: Decoration is the most important aspect of design.
items.random_wrong: Designers must follow strict government forms.
items.why: Students invert the meaning. Sullivan's modernist principle prioritises FUNCTION over ornament - the chair's shape should serve sitting, not the other way around. Postmodernism later challenged this.

items.correct: The small projecting line at the end of letter strokes.
items.misconception: A whole typeface that looks formal.
items.why: Students conflate the FEATURE (the small foot/stroke) with the CLASSIFICATION (serif typefaces, which have them). Sans serif means 'without serifs' - those little projections at letter endings.

items.q: What attitude does Pop Art take toward consumer culture?
items.correct: Ambiguous - it both celebrates and critiques mass media imagery.
items.why: Students read Pop Art as either pure celebration or pure protest. Warhol's deadpan repetition of Marilyn or soup cans is deliberately AMBIGUOUS, leaving viewers to decide if it's homage or critique.

items.q: Whose 1936 Migrant Mother is FSA's defining image?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Dorothea Lange. “Walker Evans” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Whose Dusseldorf typologies trained Gursky and Struth?
items.why: Correct answer: Becher. “Sander” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In what year did Daguerre announce his process?
items.why: The real figure is 1839 AD. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
